content.decision_question: Should Hungary pursue a structural change to the age and mechanism of academic selection, accepting higher political risk and implementation burden, or pursue narrower reforms that are more durable but address only part of the segregation problem?
content.evidence_boundary: Comparative evidence (Poland, Finland) can clarify plausible magnitudes of achievement and labour-market effects and documents concrete reversal/erosion risk, and can show which levers historically produced measurable change. It cannot resolve whether Hungary's political coalition would tolerate structural reform, nor can it determine how much weight a society should place on preserving existing institutional consensus versus achieving equity gains — that is a value judgment evidence can inform but not settle.
content.tension: Delaying selection age (T2) or piloting comprehensive de-tracking (T6) addresses the core sorting mechanism most strongly linked to inequality of opportunity, but both carry high political mobilization and reversal risk; narrower reforms (T3, T4, T5) are more feasible and legally contained but leave the age-10 selection funnel intact, meaning any equity gains are partial by construction.
content.title: Structural abolition versus incremental reform of selection
value_poles: equity of opportunity through structural change
political and institutional durability of reform
respect for existing social consensus/coalition legitimacy
